wbsp26_05_p20_explain-what-your-material:
In this assignment, I used map.jpg as a material property texture to control both the roughnessMap and metalnessMap of a sphere. In Three.js, the roughness is driven by the green channel of the texture, while the metalness is controlled by the blue channel.

When the sphere rotates, the effects of these properties become clearly visible. Areas with darker green values appear smoother and produce sharper, more noticeable highlights, while areas with brighter green values are rougher and scatter light more, resulting in softer highlights. Similarly, regions with brighter blue values appear more metallic, with stronger and more concentrated reflections, whereas darker blue areas behave more like non-metals and appear less reflective.

Because the object is rotating under a fixed light source, these differences change with the viewing angle, making the variation in material properties across the surface easy to observe.
wbsp26_05_p03_construction-description:
For this assignment, I implemented three types of articulated construction equipment: a forklift, a dump truck, and an excavator. All machines are clearly identifiable and include articulated components that can be controlled through the UI. I also applied multiple colors and materials to each vehicle to improve visual clarity and distinguish different parts.

All three machines follow a consistent design style and include functional moving components. I further enhanced the models with additional details such as wheels, lighting, and a Wisconsin “W” badge to improve visual quality and overall scene coherence. Specifically, the dump truck features a tilting bed for unloading, an opening tailgate, and rotating wheels, along with lights and warning patterns. The forklift includes a tilting mast, lifting forks, and extendable arms, as well as rotating wheels.

In addition, I designed a Mini Excavator as a third machine. This model uses a hierarchical structure with multiple degrees of freedom, including a rotating cabin, boom, stick, and bucket, enabling more complex articulated motion. Through these designs, along with added lighting, markings, and mechanical details, the overall scene is enhanced both functionally and visually, contributing to a more creative and complete result.
wbsp26_05_p21_describe-your-normal-and-b:
Normal and Bump Map Exercise Description
1. Objects and Effects

The scene contains three rotating wall objects that demonstrate different surface detail techniques:

Left (Bump Wall): Uses a grayscale bump map to create the appearance of surface height variations through lighting.
Right (Normal Wall): Uses an RGB normal map, producing more complex details and more accurate lighting variation.
Center (Combined Wall – Advanced): Uses both a color map and a bump map together.
2. How to See the Effects

To make the surface details more visible, I used two techniques:

The objects continuously rotate around the Y-axis.
A directional “grazing light” moves across the scene. When the light hits the surface at a low angle, highlights and shadows shift across the texture, making the bump and normal details clearly visible.
3. Advanced: Combined Map Coordination

The Combined Wall uses the same texture for both the color map and the bump map.

The color map provides visible surface patterns (such as cracks or stripes).
The bump map creates height variations in the exact same regions.

As a result, the visual patterns align with the lighting variations, so the cracks appear to have real depth and look more realistic.

4. Texture Attribution

Texture source: https://bitesizedtech.com/post/normal-maps-and-bump-maps-3d-art-basics-with-blender/
wbsp26_05_p02_park-description:
In this project, I completed both the standard and advanced/creative requirements for the park scene. I removed the bouncing cube and added additional playground elements, including at least one more swing and two carousels. The carousels were enhanced with poles and “horse” objects, and I implemented proper animation so that they spin while the horses move up and down.

For the advanced and creative components, I added multiple rides with hierarchical motion, including a flying chairs ride (rotating top with swinging seats), a Ferris wheel (rotating wheel with cabins that remain upright), and a drop tower ride (a rotating ring that also moves vertically). I also incorporated lighting effects on the rides to enhance the nighttime atmosphere. Additionally, I decorated the park with Easter-themed elements such as eggs, flags, and light arches, making the scene more visually engaging and thematic.
